structuralDrift.description: The 'networkStorage pool host-identity setup + reimage reclaim (interactive)' block (divider + identical 4-line prose comment + `if (-not $WhatIfPreference) { Import Test.HostIdentity; Invoke-PoolStorageSetupAndReclaim }`) is present as the final block in all three files (windows.hyper-v L80-88, macos.utm L88-96, ubuntu.kvm L269-279). Its wording is identical everywhere except ubuntu.kvm's prose has two extra sentences ('sudo is primed above so the privileged fingerprint read + mount work without a second prompt. The host fingerprint read is included in the -SudoCacheReason banner above.').
structuralDrift.explanation: The two extra sentences in ubuntu.kvm are genuinely host-specific: only the Linux/KVM path primes a sudo cache (via -SudoCacheReason) earlier in the script, so only there does the note about 'sudo is primed above' and the fingerprint read being in the sudo banner apply. Windows and macOS have no sudo-prime step, so the extra text would be false there.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the ubuntu.kvm-specific sentences. Only the shared portion of the comment (the first ~4 lines describing what the block offers) should be kept verbatim-identical across all three; it already is.

structuralDrift.description: The bulk of the executable body differs across members: ubuntu.kvm has seven additional region blocks (libvirt services + default network, yuruna image/VM storage layout, networkStorage pool SMB mount point, libvirt-qemu search ACL, GNOME idle/lock/dim, group membership probe) plus an Invoke-Step helper and an $IsLinux guard. windows.hyper-v delegates everything to Set-WindowsHostConditionSet and adds a virtual-display closing-guidance block; macos.utm delegates to Set-MacHostConditionSet and has no trailing blocks after the pool identity block.
structuralDrift.explanation: The host-preparation work (libvirt vs Hyper-V vmms vs UTM/pmset, POSIX ACLs, gsettings vs Windows power settings vs macOS defaults) is inherently platform-specific. Windows centralizes its logic in Set-WindowsHostConditionSet and macOS in Set-MacHostConditionSet, whereas the KVM host keeps its libvirt/ACL/gsettings steps inline. This is a legitimate hypervisor/OS difference, not drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: No alignment needed for the block bodies. The only cross-file consistency to enforce is the shared framing: the two blocks that DO appear in all three (the module bootstrap comment 'Shared bootstrap ... lives in automation/Yuruna.HostSetup.psm1' and the trailing networkStorage pool identity block) should stay worded identically in their shared portions, which they already do.

structuralDrift.description: Only ubuntu.kvm uses the '# -- Title ----' divider style for its inline sections and includes a '# --- See <yuruna.link>' link marker; windows.hyper-v and macos.utm have exactly one divider each (the shared networkStorage pool identity block) and no link markers.
structuralDrift.explanation: The extra dividers exist only because ubuntu.kvm keeps its host-prep steps inline rather than delegating to a single Set-*HostConditionSet call. The presence of section dividers tracks the presence of the sections, which is a justified hypervisor difference.
structuralDrift.recommendation: When normalizing to '# --- REGION:', apply the same normalization uniformly so that where the SAME block appears in multiple files (the shared networkStorage pool identity divider on windows L80, macos L88, ubuntu L269) it produces the identical REGION line. No block needs to be added or removed.

longComments.topic: Closing guidance on the opt-in virtual display (why YURUNA_VIRTUAL_DISPLAY is deliberately not set here; headless/closed-lid/KVM-yank rationale)
Why the libvirt services + default-network sudo steps are skipped when YURUNA_SUDO_PRIMED=1 (install/ubuntu.kvm.sh already ran them; avoid burning/re-prompting the sudo cache)

structuralDrift.description: windows.hyper-v has a '# === Check prerequisites ===' region (Administrator elevation + Assert-HyperVEnabled dism check) that macos.utm and ubuntu.kvm lack.
structuralDrift.explanation: Hyper-V uniquely requires an elevated (Administrator) session plus a dism-based feature check; UTM checks for the utmctl binary (L60) and KVM checks for the virsh binary (L97) and auto-relaunches under 'sg libvirt'. These are genuinely platform-specific preconditions, not a missing shared block.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the divergence, but consider giving macos.utm and ubuntu.kvm their own '# --- REGION: Check prerequisites' header over their binary-availability guards so the three share a comparable skeleton label even though the body differs.

structuralDrift.description: windows.hyper-v has a '# === Strip stale per-VM ACEs from kept base images ===' region with no counterpart in utm or kvm.
structuralDrift.explanation: Per-VM ACE accumulation on a shared base ISO hitting the ~64KB DACL limit (Add-VMDvdDrive 0x8007053C) is a Hyper-V/NTFS-only failure mode. UTM bundles and KVM per-VM directories have no equivalent shared-ISO ACE bloat.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep as-is; correctly host-specific.

structuralDrift.description: windows.hyper-v and macos.utm carry a '# === List protected base images ===' region plus base-image protection logic; ubuntu.kvm has none.
structuralDrift.explanation: On KVM base images live under ~/yuruna/image/<guest>/, a tree the scan never touches (only ~/yuruna/vms/ is scanned), so no protected-image exclusion is needed -- documented in the ubuntu.kvm .DESCRIPTION at L33-35. On Hyper-V and UTM base images share the scanned storage path and must be explicitly protected.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the divergence; it reflects a real on-disk layout difference. No action.

structuralDrift.description: ubuntu.kvm omits the '# === List registered VMs and their associated files ===' and '# === Cleanup result ===' section headers that hyper-v and utm both have, even though ubuntu.kvm contains the equivalent code (listing at L119-136, cleanup summary at L210-215).
structuralDrift.explanation: The per-VM listing loop and the final result summary are functionally the same block in all three scripts; only ubuntu.kvm is missing the header comment. This is header/skeleton drift, not a behavioral difference.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Add '# --- REGION: List registered VMs and their associated files' above ubuntu.kvm L119 and '# --- REGION: Cleanup result' above ubuntu.kvm L210 to align the skeleton with the other two members.

structuralDrift.description: ubuntu.kvm has a full comment-based-help block (<# .SYNOPSIS/.DESCRIPTION/.PARAMETER #> at L19-42) that windows.hyper-v and macos.utm lack.
structuralDrift.explanation: The comment-based-help block is a Get-Help documentation contract present only on the KVM member. It does not change behavior, but it makes only one of three siblings self-documenting -- an asymmetry rather than a justified OS/hypervisor difference.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Low priority: add an equivalent .SYNOPSIS/.DESCRIPTION help block to the hyper-v and utm members for parity, or accept the KVM one as canonical and cross-reference it from the other two.

structuralDrift.description: Major-section skeleton: win/mac carry two private preamble sections ('<host> host helpers' then 'VM lifecycle helpers') before the contract sections; KVM has a single '# === Private helpers ===' region then goes straight to the contract '# === VM lifecycle ==='.
structuralDrift.explanation: The win/mac drivers carry large private helper stacks (Hyper-V WMI/vmconnect helpers; UTM utmctl/VNC/AppleScript helpers) that warrant named sections. The KVM driver delegates almost everything to virsh through a tiny Invoke-Virsh/Get-VirshDomState pair, so a single 'Private helpers' section is proportional. The difference tracks real per-hypervisor helper volume, not accidental drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep section presence as-is. Normalize the shared section titles ('Module setup', 'Image', 'VM I/O', 'Discovery', 'Networking', 'Caching proxy', 'Host config', 'Exports') to identical REGION lines across all three; leave the host-helper-preamble sections named per host.

structuralDrift.description: The 'VM lifecycle helpers' (private) then 'VM lifecycle' (contract) two-header pattern exists in win and mac but not KVM, which has a single 'VM lifecycle' contract section.
structuralDrift.explanation: In win/mac the private *-HyperVVM* / *-UtmVM helpers sit up top and the thin contract New-VM/Start-VM/... wrappers sit lower, so two lifecycle-titled regions are genuine. On KVM the contract functions ARE the implementation (they call virsh directly), so there is only one lifecycle region. Real implementation-shape difference.
structuralDrift.recommendation: No re-order needed. When normalizing markers, keep the two win/mac lifecycle regions distinctly titled ('VM lifecycle helpers' vs 'VM lifecycle') so the private/contract split stays legible.

structuralDrift.description: 'Port-map helpers' is a dedicated major section only in the Windows driver (line 1334). macOS folds its forwarder helpers into 'macOS/UTM host helpers'; KVM has no port-map helper section (Add-PortMap/Remove-PortMap sit inline under 'Networking').
structuralDrift.explanation: Windows needs a substantial netsh-portproxy + Defender-firewall + detached-pwsh-forwarder helper stack (Get-PwshExePath, Add-CachingProxyFirewallRule, Start/Stop-WindowsCachingProxyForwarder, Clear-AllCachingProxyPortMapping), justifying its own region. macOS reuses shared Start/Stop-CachingProxyForwarder primitives declared earlier; KVM uses inline systemd socket-proxy units. Section presence tracks real per-host mechanism complexity.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep. No alignment action; the asymmetry is inherent to each host's port-forwarding mechanism.

structuralDrift.description: Sub-divider granularity differs per file: KVM uses numbered '# -- Step 1..5' plus '# -- helpers for New-YurunaExternalNetwork'; win uses '# -- Load C# type / Primary / Fallback' inside Get-HyperVScreenshot; mac uses '# --- UTM dialog watchdog' / '# --- VM lifecycle'. Each file's sub-dividers annotate functions unique to that host.
structuralDrift.explanation: These sub-dividers annotate host-specific long functions (KVM bridge build, Hyper-V screenshot capture, UTM watchdog) with no counterpart in the siblings, so there is no cross-file block to align them against.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Normalize each to '# --- REGION: <title>' in place (dropping the '# --' / trailing-dash and numbering-run variants) but do not unify titles across files — the underlying blocks are genuinely distinct.

structuralDrift.description: KVM's Networking region exports three host-only symbols (New-YurunaExternalNetwork, Get-YurunaExternalNetworkPlan, Resolve-GuestHostBinding) with no win/mac equivalent section content.
structuralDrift.explanation: Building a Linux bridge via nmcli/netplan is a KVM-only concern; Hyper-V uses Get-OrCreateYurunaExternalSwitch and macOS uses VZ-managed vmnet-shared (no operator-built bridge). The extra KVM networking symbols are legitimate host-specific surface, correctly confined to the KVM 'Networking' region and its contract list.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep as-is; no structural alignment warranted.

structuralDrift.description: KVM variants carry many more section-divider markers (libvirt-qemu ACL, Inputs, SSH key, Password hash, Yuruna host coordinates, Build apt block, Fetch CA, Render user-data, Build seed ISO, Create empty install target, Define+start VM) than Hyper-V/UTM (which only mark Seek the base image, Create copies and files for VM, Cleanup temporary folders, Guidance). The same logical steps exist in Hyper-V/UTM but are unmarked plain code.
structuralDrift.explanation: The extra KVM dividers segment the same pipeline (resolve password hash, build apt block, fetch CA cert, render user-data, build seed ISO) that Hyper-V and UTM also execute; the steps are shared, only the sectioning is not. This is presentation drift, not a hypervisor-driven difference.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Adopt one shared set of REGION headers for the common pipeline steps (Seek the base image; Create copies and files for VM; Build the autoinstall apt block; Fetch caching-proxy CA cert; Render user-data / meta-data; Build the seed ISO; Cleanup temporary folders; Guidance) and apply the same headers in all six files. Keep host-only regions (libvirt-qemu ACL, Inputs, Define+start via virt-install on KVM; Environment checks on UTM) where they are genuinely host-specific.

structuralDrift.description: UTM (both versions) has an 'Environment checks' region plus the AVF-to-QEMU memory link at the top; Hyper-V and KVM have no analogous host-precondition region marker (Hyper-V does elevation + Assert-HyperVEnabled checks but without a divider; KVM does arch/IsLinux checks under '# -- Inputs').
structuralDrift.explanation: The macOS version / Apple Silicon / UTM version gate and the AVF-to-QEMU rationale link are genuinely macOS+UTM specific host preconditions with no Hyper-V or KVM equivalent.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the UTM 'Environment checks' region and its memory link. Optionally add a lightweight host-precondition REGION header to Hyper-V (around the Administrator/Assert-HyperVEnabled block) so the three hosts read parallel, but the content stays host-specific.

structuralDrift.description: The 26.04 Hyper-V and 26.04 UTM files import Yuruna.GuestSeed.psm1 and call Build-AptProxyBlock (shared builder), dropping the inline here-string apt block AND the standalone apt-proxy-block link marker; the 24.04 Hyper-V/UTM and BOTH KVM files still build the apt block inline with a here-string and keep the link marker. KVM 26.04 keeps the link marker while using $primaryUri with the builder.
structuralDrift.explanation: The .26 Hyper-V/UTM variants were migrated to the shared Build-AptProxyBlock helper (a deliberate refactor targeting the newer guests), so the here-string is legitimately gone. However dropping the standalone link marker in .26 Hyper-V/UTM (while KVM .26 kept it) is inconsistent and is drift rather than a required consequence of the refactor.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Standardize on the Build-AptProxyBlock helper across all six files if that is the intended end state (removes the here-string drift between .24 and .26), and in every file keep a standalone '# --- REGION: https://yuruna.link/vmconfig#apt-proxy-block' link line above the Build-AptProxyBlock call so the apt-proxy-block anchor is marked uniformly.

structuralDrift.description: Marker order around CA-fetch vs Yuruna-host-coordinates vs retry-lib differs: Hyper-V/UTM compute the Yuruna host IP AFTER the CA fetch and just before the retry-lib block; KVM resolves 'Yuruna host coordinates + guest network' EARLY (before the apt block) and has no post-CA host-IP step. So the labeled steps appear in a different order per host.
structuralDrift.explanation: KVM resolves the guest network binding (Resolve-GuestHostBinding) early because networkName feeds both the apt mirror routing reasoning and the virt-install --network arg; Hyper-V/UTM resolve the reachable host IP later because switch/NAT selection happens differently. The ordering follows a real per-hypervisor data dependency.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Leave the compute ordering as-is (it is dependency-driven), but normalize the shared step names via REGION headers so a reader can see the same labeled steps in each file even when their order differs.

structuralDrift.description: Hyper-V 26.04 has an extra 'MAC-address spoofing' block (Set-VMNetworkAdapter -MacAddressSpoofing On) with multi-line rationale absent in Hyper-V 24.04; UTM 26.04 references the analogous e1000 NIC-model switch; KVM has neither.
structuralDrift.explanation: The comment explains this is a 26.04-guest-specific workaround for subiquity NetworkController CHANGE-event stalls under Hyper-V MAC-mismatch filtering, mirrored by UTM's e1000 switch. It is a genuine version+hypervisor-specific mitigation, not drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the block and its rationale in the 26.04 files only. It is correctly version-scoped; no alignment needed.

structuralDrift.description: KVM 24.04 osinfo variant candidate list is ('ubuntu24.04','ubuntu22.04'); KVM 26.04 is ('ubuntu26.04','ubuntu24.04','ubuntu22.04'), with matching wording in the fallback Write-Verbose message. Hyper-V/UTM have no osinfo block at all.

Hyper-V and UTM each carry a '# === Seek the base image ===' header and a '# === Create copies and files for VM ===' header; KVM has NEITHER header even though it performs the same base-image-seek and disk-copy/create steps (lines 71-97).

structuralDrift.description: UTM adds two extra section headers with no counterpart in Hyper-V or KVM: '# === Cloud-init seed ISO ===' (line 96) and '# === config.plist (Apple Virtualization backend) ===' (line 176).

The 'Cloud-init seed ISO' header is a candidate for family-wide adoption (see languageDrift) but the config.plist one is host-specific.

structuralDrift.description: KVM uses a trailing-dashes divider style '# -- libvirt-qemu search ACL on $HOME (self-heal) --------------------------' (line 53) for a self-heal block that has no counterpart in Hyper-V/UTM.
structuralDrift.explanation: The libvirt-qemu $HOME traverse-ACL self-heal addresses an Ubuntu-24.04-cloud-image/libvirt-qemu-user permission constraint that only exists on the KVM host; there is nothing to align in the other two files.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the block (OS-specific), but normalize the divider style to '# --- REGION: libvirt-qemu search ACL on $HOME (self-heal)' to drop the trailing dash run and match the family region convention.

structuralDrift.description: '# === Cleanup temporary folders ===' and '# === Guidance ===' trailing headers are present in Hyper-V and UTM but absent in KVM (KVM ends after virt-install with an inline Write-Verbose, no Guidance/Cleanup headers).
structuralDrift.explanation: KVM writes its seed dir under $vmDir and does not create a per-run temp seed folder that needs an explicit cleanup header, and its closing guidance is a single inline Write-Verbose. The absence reflects a real structural difference in where the seed is staged, not pure header drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Optional: for family symmetry, add a '# --- REGION: Guidance' header above the closing Write-Verbose in KVM. Cleanup header can stay omitted since KVM has no temp-folder cleanup step.

structuralDrift.description: Hyper-V and KVM include environment/precondition guards the others lack: Hyper-V has the Administrator-elevation check and Assert-HyperVEnabled (lines 42-56); KVM has the '-not $IsLinux' guard and setfacl self-heal (lines 45-67). UTM has neither.
structuralDrift.explanation: These are genuine host/OS preconditions (Windows elevation + Hyper-V feature; Linux-only + libvirt-qemu ACL). They are not comment drift and should not be aligned away.
structuralDrift.recommendation: No change. Correctly host-specific.

longComments.topic: libvirt-qemu needs a traverse (search) ACL on $HOME because Ubuntu 24.04 cloud images create /home/<user> at mode 0750, blocking the libvirt-qemu user from reaching the qcow2
qemu-img create -b truncates the backing chain to SIZE; must probe the base virtual size and pick max(base,16GiB) or the AL2023 rootfs is truncated and the guest stalls at dracut-initqueue timeout
--events on_reboot=restart is spelled out even under --import so the final 'sudo reboot now' does a QEMU system_reset (VNC socket + harness screenshot loop survive) instead of exiting
vSwitch selection must precede Get-GuestReachableHostIp because the reachable host IP is topology-dependent (Default Switch 172.x gateway vs External LAN IP via bridged NIC)

structuralDrift.description: Only the KVM file titles the SSH-key load ('# === Yuruna harness SSH key ===', line 167) and the password persistence dance ('# === Cache-VM yuruna password (cross-host persistence) ===', line 178) as region dividers. Hyper-V and macOS carry the identical logic (Get-YurunaSshPublicKey load, then the Read/Set/Get/Save-CachingProxyState password dance) as ordinary inline prose with no section header.
structuralDrift.explanation: The SSH-key load and the password persistence dance are the same logical blocks in all three files (same modules, same order-of-operations comment). KVM titles them; the other two do not. Presentational drift, not an OS/hypervisor difference.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Add matching '# --- REGION: Yuruna harness SSH key' and '# --- REGION: Cache-VM yuruna password' dividers to the Hyper-V and macOS files (or drop them from KVM). Prefer adding for a consistent region skeleton.

structuralDrift.description: The macOS file lacks a titled region for copying the boot disk (line 110, 'Copy the pre-built qcow2 ...') and for the Wi-Fi-vs-Ethernet NetworkMode decision (line 362); both run under no divider, while Hyper-V and KVM title the disk-copy and network-pick steps.
structuralDrift.explanation: The disk copy and the network selection are present in all three; macOS just omits the section headers. Missing-divider drift, not a platform difference (the underlying UTM plist/network mechanics are macOS-specific, but the region boundaries match).
structuralDrift.recommendation: Add '# --- REGION: Copy base image -> per-VM disk' (near line 110) and '# --- REGION: Pick network mode' (near line 362) to the macOS file so its region skeleton matches the siblings.

structuralDrift.description: The macOS file has no post-boot 'Start VM and wait for squid' / 'Wait for VM IP' region and no port-3128 wait loop; it ends with a '# === Guidance ===' block telling the operator to start the VM via utmctl and discover the IP manually. Hyper-V ('Start VM and wait for squid', lines 370+) and KVM ('Wait for VM IP', lines 485+) start the VM and poll IP + squid port in-script.
structuralDrift.explanation: UTM/Apple-Virtualization VMs cannot be reliably IP-discovered from the host ('utmctl ip-address' returns 'Operation not supported by the backend', per the macOS guidance lines 455-457) and the bundle is registered/started by the user through the UTM app, so the same start-and-poll loop is not possible. The Guidance region is the justified substitute.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the macOS Guidance region (justified hypervisor limitation); no wait-loop alignment needed. Normalize its divider to '# --- REGION: Guidance'.

structuralDrift.description: The KVM file has a leading '# -- libvirt-qemu search ACL on $HOME (self-heal) ---' region (line 68) and a '# === Tear down any existing VM with the same name ===' region (line 130) with no divider counterpart in the other two. Hyper-V does its existing-VM teardown inline (lines 73-94) with prose comments; macOS just Remove-Items the bundle (line 103) with an inline comment.
structuralDrift.explanation: The libvirt-qemu ACL self-heal is genuinely KVM-only (Ubuntu 24.04+ 0750 $HOME + libvirt-qemu uid traversal), so that region is justified-hypervisor. The teardown, however, is the same logical 'idempotent rebuild' step in both Hyper-V (Stop-VM/Remove-VM) and KVM (virsh destroy/undefine); only KVM titles it. macOS's teardown is a simple Remove-Item so a lighter divider is defensible.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the KVM libvirt-qemu ACL region (justified). Add a matching '# --- REGION: Tear down any existing VM' divider to the Hyper-V file (lines 73-94) so the shared rebuild step is titled in both hypervisors; leave macOS inline or add a lightweight matching divider.

structuralDrift.description: Divider style inconsistency: the KVM libvirt-qemu region uses '# -- Title ----' dash style (line 68) while every other divider in all three files uses '# === Title ===' equals style.
structuralDrift.explanation: No reason for one region in one file to use dashes with a trailing dash-run when the rest of the family uses equals. Pure stylistic drift that the REGION normalization resolves.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Normalize all dividers to '# --- REGION: Title', collapsing both the equals and the lone dash-style header into one convention.

structuralDrift.description: Only the macOS and KVM files carry the logLevel bootstrap block ('# Honor logLevel from Invoke-TestRunner.ps1 via $env:YURUNA_LOG_LEVEL. See docs/loglevels.md.' + Test.LogLevel import; macOS lines 49-51, KVM lines 52-54). The Hyper-V file has no such block.
structuralDrift.explanation: This is an inline comment rather than a divider, but it is a genuine presence difference in the block skeleton. The two POSIX-host scripts honor $env:YURUNA_LOG_LEVEL; the Hyper-V script does not, which looks like an omission rather than a Windows-specific reason.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Confirm whether the Hyper-V script should also honor logLevel; if so, add the same Test.LogLevel bootstrap. This is a logic-parity question flagged for review, not a comment-normalization fix.

phrasings: # === Start VM and wait for IP === (Hyper-V, which also covers Start-VM)
# === Wait for VM IP === (KVM)
languageDrift.suggestedCanonical: Keep distinct: Hyper-V's block starts the VM AND waits (Start-VM is inside it), so 'Start VM and wait for IP' is accurate; KVM starts the VM earlier via virt-install --import, so its block only waits. This is a genuine structural difference, not pure wording drift. If aligned to wait-only wording, KVM would use '# --- REGION: Wait for VM IP' and Hyper-V could keep 'Start VM and wait for IP'. Recommend leaving the two titles as-is because the scopes truly differ.

structuralDrift.description: The 'Remove/tear down existing VM' region ordering differs. Hyper-V places '# === Remove existing VM ===' near the top (line 62, right after Assert-HyperVEnabled, BEFORE locating the base image). KVM places '# === Tear down any existing VM with the same name ===' at line 98, AFTER locating the base image and setting up the per-VM directory/disk paths. macOS has no separate remove-existing region; it deletes the bundle inline inside '# === Create UTM bundle ===' (line 89: Remove-Item $UtmDir).
structuralDrift.explanation: Each hypervisor's teardown unit is different: Hyper-V removes a registered VM object (Stop-VM/Remove-VM by name, independent of image) so it can run first; KVM must virsh destroy+undefine a libvirt domain and it is ordered next to the per-VM disk it will overwrite; macOS UTM has no VM registry to clean, only a .utm bundle directory, so teardown is naturally folded into the bundle-creation step. The differing placement follows real per-hypervisor lifecycle differences.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the placements. Optionally normalize the two teardown titles to a shared verb, e.g. both Hyper-V and KVM use '# --- REGION: Remove existing VM' (KVM's extra 'with the same name' is redundant), so the family reads consistently even though the mechanics differ.

structuralDrift.description: macOS has a UTM-only region '# === Create UTM bundle ===' (line 88) and '# === Render config.plist from template ===' (line 180) that have no counterpart in Hyper-V or KVM. Hyper-V has a '# === Create VM disk (copy of base image) ===' region (line 111) that macOS/KVM fold into other regions (macOS copies the disk inside the bundle region; KVM copies under '# === Copy base image -> per-VM disk ===' line 111).
structuralDrift.explanation: UTM is a bundle-based hypervisor: it requires assembling a .utm directory and a config.plist descriptor, concepts that simply do not exist for Hyper-V (New-VM cmdlet) or libvirt (virt-install XML). These regions are inherently platform-specific.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep as-is. These are legitimately host-specific regions with no family-wide equivalent.

structuralDrift.description: The disk-copy step is a standalone titled region in Hyper-V ('# === Create VM disk (copy of base image) ===', line 111) and KVM ('# === Copy base image -> per-VM disk ===', line 111) but is NOT a standalone region in macOS (the & /bin/cp -c APFS-clone runs inside '# === Create UTM bundle ===').
structuralDrift.explanation: macOS deliberately co-locates the disk clone with bundle assembly because the disk must land inside the .utm/Data directory that the bundle region just created; splitting it out would separate the mkdir of Data from the copy into Data. Hyper-V and KVM write the disk into a plain per-VM directory, so a dedicated region is natural there.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Acceptable to leave. If closer alignment is desired, the two disk-copy region titles (Hyper-V 'Create VM disk (copy of base image)' vs KVM 'Copy base image -> per-VM disk') could be unified to one phrasing such as '# --- REGION: Copy base image to per-VM disk', but this is low priority.

structuralDrift.description: Network-selection region present in Hyper-V ('# === Pick a vSwitch (BEFORE the seed render) ===', line 147) and KVM ('# === Pick libvirt network (BEFORE the render) ===', line 141), absent in macOS (macOS resolves the bridge interface inline under '# === Render config.plist from template ===' via /sbin/route, lines 200-214, with no divider).
structuralDrift.explanation: Hyper-V and KVM must choose a named virtual network object (Yuruna-External vSwitch / libvirt network) whose choice feeds the baked cloud-init host coordinates, hence a pre-render region. macOS UTM derives the bridge interface from the default route at plist-render time, so the concept exists but is smaller and lives inside the plist region. The '(BEFORE the render)' parenthetical is a shared, correct hint in the two that have it.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep. Optionally give the macOS bridge-interface resolution its own '# --- REGION: Resolve bridge interface' divider so all three have an explicit network region, but this is optional.

structuralDrift.description: Hyper-V lacks a divider before the SSH-key/vault-password block, while macOS (line 114) and KVM (line 120) both have '# === Yuruna harness SSH key + vault password ==='. In Hyper-V the equivalent block (lines 133-145) is introduced only by multi-line inline prose.
structuralDrift.explanation: This block is byte-for-byte the same logic in all three files (Import Test.Ssh + Test.Extension, Get-YurunaSshPublicKey, Import-Extension authentication, Get-Password 'yuruna', the two Write-Output lines). There is no host reason for Hyper-V to omit the section header; it is pure drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Add '# --- REGION: Yuruna harness SSH key + vault password' to the Hyper-V file above line 133 (keeping the existing inline 'Shared with the caching-proxy...' rationale as a following prose comment) so all three members share the same region marker.

structuralDrift.description: Cloud-init seed-ISO generation region: Hyper-V and macOS both have '# === Generate cloud-init seed ISO ===' (line 120 / 105). KVM has no such divider; it titles its equivalent '# === Render user-data / meta-data ===' (line 131) and performs the genisoimage call under that same region without a distinct 'seed ISO' header.
structuralDrift.explanation: All three ultimately build a seed ISO (CreateIso / hdiutil makehybrid / genisoimage). Hyper-V and macOS separate 'render user-data' work from a 'Generate seed ISO' header, whereas KVM groups render + ISO under one 'Render user-data / meta-data' region. This is closer to genuine wording/structuring drift than a hypervisor constraint.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Align KVM by either renaming its region to '# --- REGION: Generate cloud-init seed ISO' or splitting out the genisoimage call under that shared header, matching Hyper-V and macOS so the family uses one consistent region name for the seed-ISO step.

Hyper-V does an elevation + Assert-HyperVEnabled check and KVM does an IsLinux + arch check inline; those are 2-3 line guards, not a multi-item section.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Acceptable to leave header-less where the checks are only a couple of lines, but for family consistency consider wrapping the Hyper-V and KVM guards in a '# --- REGION: Environment checks' so all four members open with the same skeleton.

structuralDrift.description: core-count REGION placement differs: in three files (windows.hyper-v L187, macos.utm/guest.windows.11 L210, ubuntu.kvm L164) it sits mid-script just before assigning vCPUs, but in macos.utm/guest.macos.26 (L79) it is hoisted to the top (before VMName validation).
structuralDrift.explanation: guest.macos.26 exposes -CpuCount/-MemoryMb/-DiskSizeGb as parameters and must resolve the core-count policy early to default -CpuCount and validate the >=4 vCPU floor before doing work; the other three compute cores only at the point of assignment. This is a real API-surface difference, not accidental drift.

languageDrift.concept: Skip-if-same-source / Test-DownloadAlreadyCurrent guard comment block
phrasings: windows.hyper-v & macos.utm: "Skip-if-same-source guard. Test-DownloadAlreadyCurrent (Yuruna.Host.psm1) returns $true only when $baseImageFile is on disk, the sentinel records the same URL we just resolved, and a HEAD probe's Content-Length matches the recorded byte count. The only way to force a re-download is to delete or rename $baseImageFile."

ubuntu.kvm: "The KVM host driver brings the skip-if-same-source guard + sentinel writer (Test-DownloadAlreadyCurrent / Write-ImageSentinel, the 4-line filename + URL + size + Last-Modified format shared across every KVM guest, with the noble->resolute URL-bump guard) AND the cache-aware Save-CachedHttpUri wrapper that routes this download through the squid cache."
languageDrift.suggestedCanonical: Describe the same guard identically where behavior matches. The Hyper-V/UTM wording ("Skip-if-same-source guard. Test-DownloadAlreadyCurrent ... returns $true only when ... on disk, ... same URL ..., and a HEAD probe's Content-Length matches the recorded byte count. The only way to force a re-download is to delete or rename $baseImageFile.") is the canonical description of the guard's contract; the KVM extras (Write-ImageSentinel 4-line sentinel, noble->resolute URL-bump guard, squid cache wrapper) are genuinely KVM-specific and should be kept as an additional clause rather than replacing the shared contract wording.

languageDrift.concept: Save-ImageWithChecksum checksum-verification comment block
phrasings: windows.hyper-v: "Save-ImageWithChecksum (Yuruna.Image.psm1) routes the download through Save-CachedHttpUri when available + verifies SHA-256 against the publisher checksum. A MISSING upstream checksum is a soft pass; a genuine mismatch deletes the tampered file and fails the run (WarnAndDelete), so unverified bytes never reach the base image."

macos.utm: "Save-ImageWithChecksum (Yuruna.Image.psm1) routes the download through Save-CachedHttpUri when available + verifies SHA-256 against the publisher checksum. AL2023 publishes `<basename>.qcow2.sha256` next to each qcow2; the helper parses it transparently. A genuine mismatch deletes the tampered file and fails the run (WarnAndDelete); a MISSING upstream checksum stays a soft pass (publisher lag)."

ubuntu.kvm: "Save-ImageWithChecksum (Yuruna.Image.psm1) verifies SHA-256 against the publisher checksum (a genuine mismatch deletes the file and fails; a missing upstream checksum is a soft pass). It feature-detects the driver's Save-CachedHttpUri wrapper and routes the fetch through the squid cache when one is reachable, else downloads direct."
languageDrift.suggestedCanonical: Use one canonical description of the shared Save-ImageWithChecksum contract, e.g.: "Save-ImageWithChecksum (Yuruna.Image.psm1) routes the download through Save-CachedHttpUri when available + verifies SHA-256 against the publisher checksum. A genuine mismatch deletes the tampered file and fails the run (WarnAndDelete); a MISSING upstream checksum is a soft pass." The mismatch/soft-pass semantics are identical in all three but ordered and worded differently ("deletes the tampered file" vs "deletes the file", missing-checksum clause before vs after the mismatch clause). The macOS `<basename>.qcow2.sha256` sidecar sentence and the KVM "else downloads direct" clause are format/behavior specifics that may stay as an appended note.

structuralDrift.explanation: The glyph choice (===, ---, --, boxed ====) carries no host/OS/version meaning; it is incidental authoring style within one file family, one language.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Normalize every section header to `# --- REGION: Title` and collapse the three-line `# ====...` boxes in macos.utm/guest.windows.11 to the single `# --- REGION: N. Title` line, deleting the surrounding rule-only `# ====...` lines.

structuralDrift.description: macos.utm/guest.windows.11 wraps its sections in numbered `# N) Title` boxes and adds a trailing `# Final status` block; the other three files use flat, unnumbered headers.

The Hyper-V and macos.26 scripts stage a single artifact; ubuntu.kvm stages two sequentially without a combined status gate.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the numbered structure but normalize glyphs to `# --- REGION: 1. ...` / `# --- REGION: 2. ...` / `# --- REGION: Final status`. Optionally add parallel `# --- REGION: 1./2.` headers to ubuntu.kvm for symmetry, but that is cosmetic.

structuralDrift.description: Marker style differs by language: the four bash scripts (al2023, ubuntu24, ubuntu26, macos) express every section boundary as a runtime `echo -e "\e[1;36m==== X ====\e[0m"` banner and use `# --- See <url>` only for doc pointers, whereas windows.11.update.ps1 uses `# ===== X =====` COMMENT dividers for section boundaries plus Write-Output banners for runtime output.
structuralDrift.explanation: The bash scripts intentionally emit colored ANSI banners as console OUTPUT so the host OCR/capture surface keeps repainting during a headless run; those are runtime output, not comment markers. The ps1 uses comment dividers for the same conceptual sections. This is a legitimate language/idiom split, not accidental drift, but the ps1 comment dividers still need REGION normalization.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Normalize only the ps1 `# ===== X =====` dividers to `# --- REGION: X` and the one ps1 `# --- See <url>` to `# --- REGION: <url>`. Leave the bash echo banners as runtime output; do NOT convert them into comment markers.

structuralDrift.description: Link-marker presence differs across bash members. al2023 has 3 link markers (retry-lib, powershell-yaml pwsh_retry, two-source-scheme); ubuntu24/ubuntu26 have 5 (adds 'install-powershell-first' above the PowerShell block and 'pre-extract-the-yuruna-tarball' above the tarball block); macos has only 1 (two-source-scheme).
structuralDrift.explanation: al2023 carries the same PowerShell-first and tarball-pre-extract rationale as ubuntu24/26 (inline blocks at lines 26-34 and 115-122) but lacks the corresponding `# --- See yuruna.link/memory#...` doc-pointer markers that ubuntu24/26 use. macos omits both. The retry-lib marker is legitimately absent from macos because macos sources no yuruna-retry.sh (no apt/dnf retry wrappers).
structuralDrift.recommendation: Add the two missing memory doc-pointer markers to al2023 for parity (a 'powershell-first' pointer above its PowerShell block and a 'pre-extract-the-yuruna-tarball' pointer above its tarball block), OR confirm the al2023 inline blocks intentionally stand alone. macos legitimately lacks the retry-lib marker; its omission of the other two is acceptable given its distinct inline comments. Flag for a human to decide al2023 parity.

structuralDrift.description: End-of-script tail differs. al2023 has a 'Network convergence' block plus a final `echo -e "...==== Network ready. ===="` sentinel (lines 256-303) motivated by the frozen-capture-feed idle-tail trap; ubuntu24/ubuntu26 end at the chown on line 250 with NO convergence/sentinel block; macos ends with a plain `echo "FETCHED AND EXECUTED: macos.26.update.sh"` (line 228); windows.11 ends with a cleanup block.
structuralDrift.explanation: The al2023 network-convergence + 'Network ready' end sentinel guards against the headless-Hyper-V idle-tail frozen-capture trap (a package transaction can bounce the link at its tail). ubuntu24/ubuntu26 run apt, which the al2023 comment itself says has the same transaction-tail link-bounce exposure, yet they carry neither the convergence block nor an end-of-script repaint sentinel, so their guest console can go idle before the FETCHED-AND-EXECUTED marker.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Consider porting the al2023 'Network convergence' + 'Network ready.' end sentinel (adapted for apt) to ubuntu24/ubuntu26, or confirm those guests never hit the idle-tail capture freeze. windows.11's cleanup-then-end and macos's explicit FETCHED-AND-EXECUTED line are OS-appropriate. Flag for human review.

structuralDrift.description: PowerShell-yaml install robustness differs: al2023/ubuntu24/ubuntu26 wrap the install in `pwsh_retry` with a static pre-flight log and per-attempt DNS/HEAD probes; macos does a single plain `sudo pwsh -NoProfile -Command "Install-Module ..."` with an Import smoke test but no retry; windows.11 uses an in-script 3-attempt for-loop.

There is no unjustified drift between them; the risk is only future divergence being missed because they are maintained as two copies.

structuralDrift.description: kubeadm init is retried up to 3 times (with a reset + containerd-wait between attempts) in ubuntu.server.26 (lines 257-284) but is a single unretried `sudo kubeadm init` in ubuntu.server.24 (line 256). The two files are otherwise byte-identical up to that point.
structuralDrift.explanation: This is a hardening improvement, not an OS/version-specific requirement. kubeadm init on a shared-NAT guest is the most failure-prone network-touching step; the 26 variant wraps it in the same retry-with-reset pattern used elsewhere in the script, while 24 leaves it as a single-shot call that aborts the whole install under `set -euo pipefail` on any transient blip. Nothing about Ubuntu 24 vs 26 changes kubeadm's failure surface, so the two files should carry the same logic.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Backport the ubuntu.server.26 kubeadm_init retry loop (lines 262-284) into ubuntu.server.24, replacing the single line 256 `sudo kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16`. After that the two guest scripts should be identical except for genuinely version-specific bits.

structuralDrift.description: The Windows .ps1 installs additional tooling the Ubuntu scripts do not (Cloud CLIs: Azure/AWS/Google Cloud, Graphviz, GitHub CLI) and omits containerd cache-mirror config, Flannel manifest apply, swap disable, kubeadm init, and kernel-module/sysctl setup present in the Ubuntu scripts.
structuralDrift.explanation: Windows 11 uses Docker Desktop's built-in single-node Kubernetes (enabled via Settings), so there is no kubeadm/containerd/Flannel/swap/sysctl bring-up to perform in the script -- those are Linux control-plane concerns handled by Docker Desktop on Windows. The extra Cloud CLIs / Graphviz / GitHub CLI are winget-delivered workstation tooling; the Ubuntu server guests provision a headless cluster node. These are genuine platform differences, not drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep as-is. No alignment needed; the divergence reflects the Docker-Desktop-managed cluster model on Windows versus kubeadm bring-up on the Ubuntu server guests.

structuralDrift.description: The Flannel/OpenTofu/Helm/mkcert network-hardening 'why' comment blocks appear in an identical order in both .sh files, but the .ps1 has no equivalent comments because it installs everything through winget rather than raw curl fetches.

The Windows script installs via winget, which handles its own retry/mirror behavior, so those failure classes and their comments do not apply. Absence on Windows is correct, not missing coverage.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep as-is.

structuralDrift.description: Node.js installation block: AL2023 installs Node via the NodeSource RPM setup script plus 'dnf -y install nodejs' (echo banner '==== Node.js ===='), while both Ubuntu files install Node via NVM inside a heredoc (echo banner '==== NVM and Node.js ====').
structuralDrift.explanation: Amazon Linux 2023 uses dnf/RPM and the NodeSource RPM repo is the idiomatic path there; Debian/Ubuntu use NVM. These are genuine per-OS package-management mechanisms, not accidental drift. The differing echo banner ('Node.js' vs 'NVM and Node.js') correctly reflects the different mechanism and is runtime output, not a comment marker.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the OS-specific install mechanisms and their matching echo banners as-is. No alignment needed.

structuralDrift.description: Post-install cross-user symlink block and NVM-aware Installation Summary: both Ubuntu files add a '/usr/local/bin' symlink step for node/npm/n8n (lines 48-54) and run the summary inside an NVM-sourced 'bash -c' subshell (lines 58-64); AL2023 has neither, running a plain summary (lines 38-41).
structuralDrift.explanation: The symlinking and NVM-sourced summary exist only because NVM installs Node under a per-user $HOME/.nvm path that is not on the global PATH; AL2023's dnf install places node/npm on the system PATH already, so no symlinks or NVM sourcing are required. The presence difference follows directly from the OS-specific install mechanism.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the divergence. It is a consequence of the NVM-vs-dnf choice, not unjustified drift.

structuralDrift.description: Non-interactive env exports (DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive, NONINTERACTIVE=1) appear in both Ubuntu files (lines 7-9) but are absent from the AL2023 file.
structuralDrift.explanation: DEBIAN_FRONTEND is a Debian/apt-specific variable with no effect on Amazon Linux's dnf; omitting it from AL2023 is correct. NONINTERACTIVE=1 is a Homebrew/nvm convenience that pairs with the Ubuntu NVM path.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep as-is; the exports are meaningful only on the Debian/Ubuntu path.

structuralDrift.description: The ubuntu.server.24 and ubuntu.server.26 files are byte-for-byte identical (both 65 lines, same content).

The Ubuntu server images intentionally do not install a GUI desktop group in this OpenClaw script.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Leave as-is; do not force a GUI Desktop block into the Ubuntu scripts. If desktop parity is actually desired on Ubuntu, that is a product decision to be made explicitly, not a comment-drift alignment.

structuralDrift.description: Node.js install differs: AL2023 uses NodeSource rpm setup ('rpm.nodesource.com/setup_${YURUNA_NODE_MAJOR}.x' then 'dnf -y install nodejs') under a '==== Node.js ====' banner; Ubuntu members use NVM ('==== NVM and Node.js ====' banner) inside a heredoc.

structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep both mechanisms. The banners are runtime output (not comment markers), so no region normalization applies; the wording correctly reflects the different tooling.

The DEBIAN_FRONTEND/NONINTERACTIVE exports are Debian/apt-specific. All are OS-mechanism-driven, not accidental drift.

structuralDrift.description: Ordering of the 'stop PostgreSQL and wait for shutdown' block differs relative to package install. In AL2023 the stop-loop runs BEFORE the dnf install (lines 28-35, then install line 38). In both Ubuntu files the stop-loop runs AFTER the apt install of postgresql-18 (install lines 33-39, then stop-loop lines 41-48).
structuralDrift.explanation: On AL2023 the postgresql17-server package is not yet installed on first run, so the stop check is a defensive no-op that harmlessly precedes install; the initdb/data-clear step (lines 40-42) is what requires the service stopped. On Ubuntu the postgresql service only exists after the postgresql-18 package is installed, so the stop-and-drop-cluster logic must come after install. structuralDrift.description: Uniform entry-point skeleton (prelude import -> Initialize-YurunaEntryPoint -> Initialize-YurunaEntryPointModuleSet -For PoolAdmin -> exit-code fetch -> powershell-yaml import -> Resolve-YurunaPoolAdminTarget -> URL guard -> Open-YurunaPoolIntent -> Read-YurunaPoolsDoc) is identical across all seven files.
structuralDrift.explanation: All seven scripts share one bootstrap block byte-for-byte (only differing by the CmdletBinding attribute set and per-script parameters). This is the intended common contract for the pool-admin CLI family, not drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: No change. Keep the shared skeleton in lockstep; if it ever needs editing, edit all seven together.

structuralDrift.description: SupportsShouldProcess / -Confirm posture differs: the five write scripts (New-Pool, Add-HostToPool, Remove-HostFromPool, Set-PoolDesiredState, Set-PoolTestSet) use [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess)]; the two read-only scripts (Get-PoolStatus, Test-PoolIntent) use bare [CmdletBinding()].
structuralDrift.explanation: Get-PoolStatus and Test-PoolIntent never write/commit, so they correctly omit SupportsShouldProcess. This is a deliberate read-only vs write distinction, not accidental drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: No change. The presence/absence of SupportsShouldProcess correctly tracks whether the script mutates the intent store.

structuralDrift.description: Input-validation regex guard is present in New-Pool (PoolId), Add-HostToPool (HostId), and Set-PoolTestSet (Name), but absent in Remove-HostFromPool, Set-PoolDesiredState, Get-PoolStatus, and Test-PoolIntent.
structuralDrift.explanation: New-Pool validates PoolId with a DNS-label regex and Add-HostToPool validates HostId with the 42-prefix regex. Remove-HostFromPool takes the same HostId parameter but does NOT validate it before use; because removal is a membership diff, an invalid HostId simply matches nothing (no corruption), so the omission is arguably safe. Set-PoolDesiredState relies on ValidateSet for State and needs no PoolId regex because it only looks up an existing pool. Get-PoolStatus/Test-PoolIntent are read-only. This is mostly justified, but the asymmetry between Add-HostToPool (validates HostId) and Remove-HostFromPool (does not) is a genuine inconsistency worth a decision.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Decide the HostId-validation contract once and apply it to both Add-HostToPool and Remove-HostFromPool identically -- either both validate the 42-prefixed 32-hex format up front, or neither does and both rely on the membership set-diff being harmless for bad input. Do not leave one validating and the other not.

structuralDrift.description: Repair-CachingProxyForwarder.ps1 uses a 'Step 1 / Step 2 / Step 3' numbered scheme for its three sections, while Test-Sequence.ps1 (the other multi-stage entry point in the family) uses unnumbered titled '# === ... ===' regions. Remove-TestVMFiles.ps1 also uses unnumbered titles.
structuralDrift.explanation: Repair-CachingProxyForwarder.ps1 is a macOS/UTM-only linear procedure whose three numbered steps are also echoed to the operator as runtime banners ('== Step 1: ... ==', line 111) so the operator can correlate console output to the numbered comment. The numbered scheme is intentional and matches the user-facing output. Test-Sequence.ps1 and Remove-TestVMFiles.ps1 are not linear-step procedures.
structuralDrift.recommendation: Keep the numbered '1./2./3.' inside each region title (the convention allows numbered regions: '# --- REGION: Step 1: ...'). Do not force it onto the other two files, which are not linear step procedures.

structuralDrift.description: New-LocalTestUser.ps1 and Repair-CachingProxyForwarder.ps1 carry a full comment-based help block (.SYNOPSIS/.DESCRIPTION/.PARAMETER/.EXAMPLE) after the PSScriptInfo header; Test-Sequence.ps1 has one too. Remove-TestVMFiles.ps1 has NO comment-based help block -- it jumps straight from the PSScriptInfo/#requires to param().
structuralDrift.explanation: Remove-TestVMFiles.ps1 is a documented operator entry point (it takes -Prefix / -Quiet, is invoked both by the cycle runner and directly by the operator) yet lacks the .SYNOPSIS/.PARAMETER help the three siblings all provide. This is a presence gap, not an OS/hypervisor/version difference -- the -Quiet parameter's behavior is instead buried in an inline param() comment (lines 21-29).
structuralDrift.recommendation: Add a comment-based help block (.SYNOPSIS/.DESCRIPTION/.PARAMETER Prefix/.PARAMETER Quiet/.EXAMPLE) to Remove-TestVMFiles.ps1 to match the family; the -Quiet rationale currently inline in param() can move into .PARAMETER Quiet.

structuralDrift.description: Ordering of the host-detection region relative to the driver-wiring region differs. In Remove-TestVMFiles.ps1, Get-HostType is called early (line 80) before Test-HostRequirement and Initialize-YurunaHost. In Test-Sequence.ps1 the '# === Detect host ===' region (line 210) comes after config load and status-server start, deliberately BEFORE sequence resolution.
structuralDrift.explanation: Test-Sequence.ps1 explicitly documents (lines 211-214) that HostType must be resolved before sequence resolution so Resolve-SequencePath can prefer a per-host sequence variant -- a Test-Sequence-specific need that Remove-TestVMFiles.ps1 (which only enumerates VMs by prefix) does not have. The ordering difference reflects genuinely different responsibilities, not drift.
structuralDrift.recommendation: No change. Keep both orderings; they are driven by each script's distinct data dependencies.
